response: Taking effect in May 2018, the EU’s General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) applies to any organization handling EU residents’ data and enshrines rights to access, erasure, portability and explicit consent, while requiring breach disclosures within 72 hours and Data Protection Officers for large-scale processors. Violations can cost up to 4 % of global annual revenue or €20 million, prompting landmark fines such as €1.2 billion for Meta, €746 million for Amazon and more than €200 million for Google. The GDPR has become the template for privacy statutes worldwide—informing California’s CCPA, Brazil’s LGPD and India’s Digital Personal Data Protection Act—yet critics warn its compliance burdens hurt small firms, spawn ubiquitous cookie banners and suffer from uneven enforcement. Despite these drawbacks, it solidified personal data protection as a fundamental right and set the global standard for modern privacy legislation.
